title: Long Addition Calculator - Column Addition Steps
tagline: Add two whole numbers and see the carries, aligned columns, and step-by-step working instantly.
intro: Enter two whole numbers to generate a clean column-addition layout and a short explanation for each place value.

error.invalid: Enter two whole numbers to use long addition.

result.heading: Result
result.stepCarry: At the {place} place, {a} + {b} = {total}. Write {digit} and carry {carryOut}.
result.stepIncomingCarry: At the {place} place, {a} + {b} + carried {carryIn} = {total}. Write {digit} and carry {carryOut}.

about.heading: About the long addition calculator
about.body:
Long addition is the standard place-value method for adding multi-digit numbers by lining up the digits in columns. Ones go under ones, tens under tens, hundreds under hundreds, and so on. You start on the right, add one column at a time, write the digit that belongs in that column, and carry any extra value to the next column on the left. This is the method taught in elementary math because it is reliable, transparent, and easy to scale from short sums to very large numbers.

This long addition calculator focuses on whole numbers so the structure of the algorithm stays clear. After you enter the two numbers, the tool aligns the digits automatically and shows a carry row whenever a column total is 10 or more. That is especially useful for learners who understand basic addition facts but still need help seeing where each carried 1 comes from. The display mirrors the written format teachers often expect on worksheets, homework, and classroom assessments.

The logic behind carrying is simple place value. If a column adds to 15, you write the 5 in the current place and carry the 1 to the next column because 15 ones equals 1 ten and 5 ones. The same pattern repeats in every column. Once students understand that carrying is just regrouping across place values, long addition becomes much less mysterious. Adults also benefit from seeing the structure when checking bookkeeping totals, invoice sums, or exam practice without reaching immediately for mental math.

A tool like this is valuable because it does more than output a final answer. It also makes the process visible. If you entered 458 and 267, you can see the ones column produce 15, the carried 1 affecting the tens column, and the next carried 1 affecting the hundreds column. That kind of breakdown is helpful for review, tutoring, homeschooling, and self-study because it turns a finished total into a worked example.

Use the long addition calculator when you want both speed and clarity. It can confirm homework, demonstrate the algorithm to a student, or serve as a quick check when you are adding by hand and want to be sure each carry landed in the right place. The answer matters, but understanding why the answer is correct is what makes long addition such a foundational skill.

examples.heading: Long addition examples
examples.intro: Here are a few everyday addition problems that show how carrying works across columns.

items.note: The ones column makes 15, so you write 5 and carry 1 before finishing the tens and hundreds columns.
Every column creates a carry, so the final answer extends to a new thousands place.
Zero in the tens place does not change the method; you still add by aligned place value.
When no carrying is needed in a column, you simply write the direct sum for that place.

howto.heading: How to use the long addition calculator
steps: Enter the first whole number in the First Number field.
Enter the second whole number in the Second Number field.
Click Calculate to see the total, the aligned column layout, and the carry row.
Read the step-by-step explanation to check each place value from right to left.
Use Reset to clear the form before trying another problem.

items.a: Long addition is a place-value method that adds numbers one column at a time from right to left while carrying extra value to the next column when needed. It is the standard written algorithm taught in elementary school because it makes each step of the addition process transparent.

items.q: Why do we carry in addition?
items.a: You carry because ten ones become one ten, ten tens become one hundred, and so on. Carrying is just regrouping by place value.

items.q: Do I need to line up digits by place value?
items.a: Yes. Correct alignment is essential because each column must represent the same place value in both numbers. Misaligned digits would cause tens to be added to ones, or hundreds to tens, making the result completely wrong.

items.q: Can this help with homework checking?
items.a: Yes. The calculator shows the final sum and the intermediate carries, which makes it useful for checking hand-written work. You can compare your carry row to the one the tool generates to spot exactly where a mistake occurred.

items.q: Why does the answer sometimes have more digits than both inputs?
items.a: If the leftmost column also produces a carry, the final answer gains a new digit, such as 999 + 1 = 1000. This is a natural consequence of place value, where ten units in one position regroup into one unit of the next higher position.
